name: Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua Trail
name_original: Vía verde de los Molinos del Agua – Pista Antiguo Ferrocarril de Buitrón
description: Mountain bike the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua Trail, a moderate 34.5-mile route through Huelva's historical landscapes and varied fore
region_intro: Mountain biking around Valverde Del Camino offers a diverse landscape characterized by extensive networks of trails. The region features a mix of natural paths, compacted dirt tracks, gravel, and singletrack sections, traversing pine forests, cork oak meadows, and areas with eucalyptus. Riverine scenery along the Tinto and Odiel rivers, alongside greenways built on old railway lines, provides varied riding experiences. This terrain, shaped by historical mining activity, includes significant climbs and descents, making it suitable for various skill levels. Best mountain bike trails around Valverde Del Camino The most popular mountain bike trail is Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua Trail, a 34.5 miles (55.5 km) moderate trail that takes 3 hours 18 minutes to complete, following a scenic greenway. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Valverde del Camino, Embalse de Calabazar, Calañas, Zalamea la Real – circular route, a difficult 48.1 miles (77.4 km) path. This route features significant elevation changes and passes by the Embalse de Calabazar reservoir. Local mountain bikers also love the Río Odiel, Embalse de Calabazar, Vía Verde del Camino – ruta circular, a 15.1 miles (24.3 km) trail leading through riverine landscapes and past the Embalse de Calabazar, often completed in about 2 hours 20 minutes. Mountain biking around Valverde Del Camino is defined by diverse forests, riverine scenery, and unique mining heritage landscapes. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to different ability levels. The routes in Valverde Del Camino are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 30 reviews. More than 500 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Valverde Del Camino's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available around Valverde del Camino?
A: There are over 60 mountain bike trails around Valverde del Camino, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. This includes 9 easy routes, 33 moderate routes, and 19 difficult routes, ensuring there's something for everyone.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on mountain bike trails in Valverde del Camino?
A: The terrain around Valverde del Camino is diverse, featuring a mix of natural paths, compacted dirt tracks, gravel, and singletrack sections. You'll ride through pine forests, cork oak meadows, and areas with eucalyptus, often encountering significant climbs and descents due to the region's historical mining activity.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in the area?
A: Yes, Valverde del Camino offers several circular mountain bike routes. A challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995562991/valverde-del-camino-embalse-de-calabazar-calanas-zalamea-la-real-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde del Camino, Embalse de Calabazar, Calañas, Zalamea la Real – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 77.4 km. For a moderate, shorter ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995750007/rio-odiel-embalse-de-calabazar-via-verde-del-camino-ruta-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Odiel, Embalse de Calabazar, Vía Verde del Camino – ruta circular&lt;/a&gt;, covering 24.3 km.

Q: What natural landmarks or attractions can I see along the mountain bike trails?
A: Many routes incorporate natural and historical landmarks. You can ride past the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301124" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calabazar Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, explore the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5765536" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dolmens of El Pozuelo&lt;/a&gt;, or cross the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301118" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman bridge over the Odiel River&lt;/a&gt;. The region's mining heritage also means you'll encounter unique landscapes shaped by historical activity.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in Valverde del Camino?
A: The best time for mountain biking in Valverde del Camino is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant for outdoor activities, avoiding the intense heat of summer and potential winter rains.

Q: Are there any family-friendly mountain bike trails suitable for beginners?
A: Yes, there are 9 easy routes around Valverde del Camino that are suitable for families and beginners. These trails typically involve less elevation gain and shorter distances, making them a good starting point for those new to mountain biking or riding with children.

Q: Are there any trails that follow old railway lines or greenways?
A: Absolutely. The region is known for its greenways built on old railway lines. A prominent example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995497735/via-verde-de-los-molinos-del-agua-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 55.5 km route that follows a scenic greenway. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5346190" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde Molino de Acha&lt;/a&gt; is another notable greenway in the area.

Q: What do other mountain bikers say about the trails in Valverde del Camino?
A: The mountain bike trails in Valverde del Camino are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 30 reviews. Riders often praise the diverse landscapes, the mix of natural paths and greenways, and the historical elements integrated into the routes.

Q: Are there options for very challenging mountain bike rides?
A: Yes, for experienced mountain bikers seeking a challenge, there are 19 difficult routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24522765" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dolmens of El Pozuelo – Tronconcito Climb loop from Valverde del Camino&lt;/a&gt;, which is 80.7 km long with significant elevation changes, demanding very good fitness and advanced riding skills.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through riverine landscapes?
A: Many routes in Valverde del Camino feature beautiful riverine scenery. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995750007/rio-odiel-embalse-de-calabazar-via-verde-del-camino-ruta-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Odiel, Embalse de Calabazar, Vía Verde del Camino – ruta circular&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, leads through riverine landscapes and past the Embalse de Calabazar.

Q: Are there any long-distance mountain bike routes available?
A: Yes, the region offers several long-distance routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19325361" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde Molino de Acha – Columbus House, Huelva loop from Valverde del Camino&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 94.5 km route, providing an extensive ride through varied landscapes.
tips: There are several places that can be visited to discover more of this town. For example, the Ermita del Santo, the old railway station (where you can see a wonderful Berlina car from 1900) and the Los Gabrieles dolmen complex, which can be seen on the outskirts of town. The most outstanding festivals are the Romería de San Pancracio in June and the August Fair during the week of August 15.

It goes from the banks of the Tinto to the ravines of the Serranía de Huelva with a length of 33.2km and a firm of asphalt and compacted gravel. The route passes through countryside, pine forests, cork oak meadows and eucalyptus repopulations. Its name comes from the group of Water Mills in Beas (flour mills from the 17th, 18th and 19th centuries).

Its main productive activity is the cultivation of cereals, sunflowers, olive trees, citrus fruits, legumes, and the breeding of sheep, pigs and wild cattle. Its important monuments include the Dolmen de Soto, the Church of San Antón, the Convent of Carmen, the Jesuit College and the Hermitage of Santa Misericordia.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail primarily features a mixed surface of asphalt and compacted gravel, with some sections of compacted earth. As it follows an old railway line, it generally has gentle gradients and minimal slopes, making for a relatively smooth ride.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to mountain bike this trail?
faqs.answer: The full route is approximately 55.5 kilometers (34.5 miles) long. For mountain biking, it typically takes around 3 hours and 18 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Yes, while rated as 'moderate' for mountain biking, its generally flat profile and well-maintained surface make it accessible for various ages and skill levels. It's also suitable for families and people with reduced mobility.

faqs.question: What historical or natural sights can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route is rich in history and nature. You'll pass by several 17th, 18th, and 19th-century flour mills in Beas, some of which have been restored. The trail also follows the path of the old El Buitrón mining railway, with informative notices about its heritage. Natural scenery includes pine forests, cork oak and eucalyptus trees, olive groves, and sunflower fields. Notable highlights include the Valverde del Camino Town Center, Vía Verde Molino de Acha, and the Dolmen de Soto.

faqs.question: Where can I access the trail and find parking?
faqs.answer: The trail connects several towns, including San Juan del Puerto, Trigueros, Beas, and Valverde del Camino. You can access it from various points in these towns, often near old railway stations or designated rest areas. Parking availability will vary by access point, typically found within the towns themselves.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to mountain bike this route?
faqs.answer: To enjoy the trail comfortably, consider visiting during spring or autumn. These seasons offer milder temperatures, which are ideal for cycling, and you can experience the diverse landscapes at their most vibrant.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the trail?
faqs.answer: Generally, Vías Verdes in Spain are dog-friendly, but it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash to ensure the safety and comfort of all users and to protect local wildlife.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to use the trail?
faqs.answer: No, Vías Verdes are typically free to access and do not require permits. They are public recreational routes converted from old railway lines.

faqs.question: Does the trail connect with other routes?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Camino de Santiago del Sur and the Transandalus.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the trail?
faqs.answer: The diverse natural environments along the route offer a chance to observe local wildlife. You might spot various bird species such as Crested Larks, Black Kites, and Common Buzzards.

faqs.question: Are there facilities like rest areas or water points along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail includes useful infrastructure such as designated rest areas and passes through old railway stations in the towns it connects, which may offer additional facilities.
